info.text: This sketch demonstrates a few key principles of the img element. Though most elements have an opening and closing tag, img is one of those few elements known as a "singleton" or "void element" which only consists of a single opening tag.
info.title: getting started
img attributes
info.text: This img element has two attributes, the first is src which we use to specify the image file we want to place in this element, and the second is alt which is used to provide alternative information for an image if a user for some reason cannot view it (we'll come back to this later).

info.title: two files, many imgs
info.text: We've got lots of img elements in this demo (22 to be exact), but they all use just two source files, the first is called dancing-girl.gif and the second is called dancing-girl-bw.gif. By adding more img tags you can instantiate either image as many times as you like. These image files have a .gif at the end of their names which means they're GIFs (I'll let you pronounce that however you want).

info.title: GIF "spirit of the web"
info.text: The GIF is a very special image file format, what net artist Olia Lialina has called "the spirit of the web". It was the first format supported by the img element when it was first introduced. Despite its limitations (or maybe because of them) it's remained one of the most popular formats for online content, embraced by early web makers to formal artists to all manner of creative folks in between.

info.title: image formats
info.text: There are other image file formats we can assign to an img element's src, each has its own pros and cons. Generally speaking, you want to choose an image format that will look good while also being as small as possible.

info.title: file size
info.text: When I say small I don't mean the dimensions (width/height) of the image, I mean the amount of data stored in the image file. This gif is 5KB (five kilobytes) which is very small, so it transfers quickly over the network and loads almost instantly for viewers. Ideally our media assets (images, videos, audio, etc) would be less than 5MB (five megabytes) each, that's 5,000 kilobytes.

info.title: JPG
info.text: GIF's tend to be real small, even when animated like these, but they're not great at storing high resolution images. If I wanted to include a photograph I took on my page, it would be wiser to use a JPG (aka JPEG), which are a great at keeping photos looking good while keeping the file size small.

info.title: PNG
info.text: A GIF can contain colored pixels but also transparent pixels, a JPG however can only contain colored pixels. If you have a high resolution image that needs to look crisp but also has transparent parts, then you're going to want to use a PNG. Like GIFS, PNG's can also be animated and they actually support varying degrees of opacity. GIFs do not, each pixel is either fully transparent or fully opaque. Like JPGs, PNGs can display photos perfectly, but the trade-off is going to be size. PNGs are usually much larger than GIF and JPG files, so only use them if you have to.

info.title: other formats
info.text: These are the most common image file types, but there are others the img element supports like AVIF, the increasingly popular WebP and the infinitely scalable (meaning you can make it as big or small as you want without loosing resolution) SVG, we'll talk more about those in other lessons.

info.title: img's are inline
info.text: The img element isn't the only element in this demo. Notice that there are 4 rows of visible images in the rendered page. img are "inline" elements, which means that when we write them one after another, as we've done here, they'll show up on the same line, meaning next to each other on the page.

info.title: br element
info.text: So in order to break them up into separate rows I've added br elements between every 5 images. A br element is used to create a line break in text, also known as a carriage-return. Because images show up "in-line", like text does, a br can also be used to create a line break between images.

info.title: broken imgs
info.text: Now, take a look at this last row, which only contains two img elements, neither of which are showing up because I've intentionally misspelled the file's name (I left out the "i" in "girl"), so the browser couldn't locate the asset.

info.title: missing alt
info.text: This first img is missing its alt attribute so it shows up on the page as a "broken image" icon.

info.title: Chuck Poynter
info.text: This second img does have an alt attribute, so the page renders that instead of the broken image icon, in this case a short description and the author's name, Chuck Poynter.

info.title: alt's purpose
info.text: But this isn't the only function of the alt attribute. It's also used by assistive technologies, like screen readers, to read aloud this description for visually impaired users. Because of this, it's considered "best practice" and also has an impact on your SEO, or search engine optimization. This is why, although it's not technically necessary I'll make sure to warn you with an orange dot on any lines containing img elements missing their alt attribute.

info.title: next demo
info.text: Feel free to experiment with this demo, or we can jump to another media element demo, the audio element.
